title: Cecil Hepworth

infobox.value: (19 March 1874 – 9 February 1953) was a British [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director"), [producer](/wiki/Film_producer "Film producer") and [screenwriter](/wiki/Screenwriter "Screenwriter"). He was among the founders of the [British film industry](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cinema of the United Kingdom") and continued making films into the 1920s at his [Hepworth Studios](/wiki/Hepworth_Studios "Hepworth Studios"). In 1923 his company [Hepworth Picture Plays](/wiki/Hepworth_Pictures "Hepworth Pictures") went into [receivership](/wiki/Receivership "Receivership").

Mills, Ted (31 March 2016). ["The First Film Adaptation of Alice in Wonderland (1903)"](http://www.openculture.com/2016/03/the-earliest-film-adaptation-of-alice-in-wonderland-from-1903.html). *Open Culture*. Retrieved 19 May 2017.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British film director, producer and screenwriter (1874-1953)

| Cecil Hepworth | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Born | (1874-03-19)19 March 1874  [Lambeth](/wiki/Lambeth "Lambeth"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) England |
| Died | 9 February 1953(1953-02-09) (aged 78)  [Greenford](/wiki/Greenford "Greenford"), [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex "Middlesex"), England |
| Occupations | Film director, film producer |
| Years active | 1896–1926 |

**Cecil Milton Hepworth** (19 March 1874 – 9 February 1953) was a British [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director"), [producer](/wiki/Film_producer "Film producer") and [screenwriter](/wiki/Screenwriter "Screenwriter"). He was among the founders of the [British film industry](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cinema of the United Kingdom") and continued making films into the 1920s at his [Hepworth Studios](/wiki/Hepworth_Studios "Hepworth Studios"). In 1923 his company [Hepworth Picture Plays](/wiki/Hepworth_Pictures "Hepworth Pictures") went into [receivership](/wiki/Receivership "Receivership").

His works include *[Alice in Wonderland](/wiki/Alice_in_Wonderland_(1903_film) "Alice in Wonderland (1903 film)")* (1903), the first film adaptation of [Lewis Carroll](/wiki/Lewis_Carroll "Lewis Carroll")'s children's book *[Alice's Adventures in Wonderland](/wiki/Alice%27s_Adventures_in_Wonderland "Alice's Adventures in Wonderland")*.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cecil_Hepworth&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Vivaphone_ad_1913.jpg/250px-Vivaphone_ad_1913.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vivaphone_ad_1913.jpg)

1913 ad for Vivaphone

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Norman_Whitten_Mad_Hatter_1903.jpg/250px-Norman_Whitten_Mad_Hatter_1903.jpg)](/wiki/File:Norman_Whitten_Mad_Hatter_1903.jpg)

[May Clark](/wiki/May_Clark "May Clark") as Alice (left) and [Norman Whitten](/wiki/Norman_Whitten "Norman Whitten") (right) as the Mad Hatter in *[Alice in Wonderland](/wiki/Alice_in_Wonderland_(1903_film) "Alice in Wonderland (1903 film)")* (1903), the first film version of the story

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Cecil_Hepworth_Film_Cell_Plaque_London_NW1_9XT.jpg/250px-Cecil_Hepworth_Film_Cell_Plaque_London_NW1_9XT.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cecil_Hepworth_Film_Cell_Plaque_London_NW1_9XT.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Cecil_Hepworth_-_32_Cantelowes_Road_London_NW1_9XT.jpg/120px-Cecil_Hepworth_-_32_Cantelowes_Road_London_NW1_9XT.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cecil_Hepworth_-_32_Cantelowes_Road_London_NW1_9XT.jpg)

A commemorative [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute") plaque at [Cantelowes Road](/wiki/Cantelowes_(ward) "Cantelowes (ward)"), NW1, where Hepworth lived as a child

Hepworth was born in [Lambeth](/wiki/Lambeth "Lambeth"), in present-day [South London](/wiki/South_London "South London"). His father, Thomas Cradock Hepworth, was a famous [magic lantern](/wiki/Magic_lantern "Magic lantern") showman and author. Cecil Hepworth became involved in the early stages of British filmmaking, working for both [Birt Acres](/wiki/Birt_Acres "Birt Acres") and [Charles Urban](/wiki/Charles_Urban "Charles Urban"), and wrote the first British book on the subject in 1897. With his cousin Monty Wicks he set up the production company Hepworth and Co. (also known as "Hepwix" after the word mark in its trade logo), which was later renamed the Hepworth Manufacturing Company (officially: Hepworth Film Manufacturing Company), and then [Hepworth Picture Plays](/wiki/Hepworth_Pictures "Hepworth Pictures"). In 1899 they built a small [film studio](/wiki/Film_studio "Film studio") in [Walton-on-Thames](/wiki/Walton-on-Thames "Walton-on-Thames"), [Hepworth Studios](/wiki/Hepworth_Studios "Hepworth Studios"). The company produced about three films a week, sometimes with Hepworth directing. He was associated with [Percy Stow](/wiki/Percy_Stow "Percy Stow") from 1901-1903 who specialized in trick films.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

His film *[Rescued by Rover](/wiki/Rescued_by_Rover "Rescued by Rover")* (1905), co-directed with Lewin Fitzhamon and starring a [collie](/wiki/Collie "Collie") in the title role, was a huge financial success. The film is now regarded as an important development in [film grammar](/wiki/Film_grammar "Film grammar"), with shots being effectively combined to emphasize the action. Hepworth was also one of the first to recognize the potential of [film stars](/wiki/Movie_star "Movie star"), both animal and human, with several recurring characters appearing in his films.

By 1910, Hepworth was also the inventor of Vivaphone, an early sound on disk system for adding sound to motion pictures. The device used phonograph records to record and play back the sound. Hepworth's Vivaphone was distributed in Britain and also in the United States and Canada.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The company continued making popular films into the 1920s, despite Hepworth's now unchanging and increasingly old-fashioned film style. Boosted by the international success of *[Alf's Button](/wiki/Alf%27s_Button_(1920_film) "Alf's Button (1920 film)")* (1919), the company went [public](/wiki/Public_company "Public company") to fund a large studio development. He failed to raise the necessary capital and, also suffering the box office failure of *[Comin' Thro the Rye](/wiki/Comin%27_Thro_the_Rye_(1923_film) "Comin' Thro the Rye (1923 film)")* (1923), the company went into [receivership](/wiki/Receivership "Receivership") the next year. All of the original film negatives in Hepworth's possession were melted down by the receiver in order to sell the silver, and his feature films have been considered [lost](/wiki/Lost_film "Lost film") for many decades. However, an original 35mm. print of his 1920 film *[Helen of Four Gates](/wiki/Helen_of_Four_Gates "Helen of Four Gates")* was located in a film archive in [Montreal, Quebec](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") in 2008.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The [Cecil Hepworth Playhouse](/wiki/Walton_Studios#Closure_and_demolition "Walton Studios") in Walton-on-Thames is named after him.
